title: One-two punch delivered in global operation disrupts cybercrime "assembly line"
gist: Operation Endgame—a coordinated international law enforcement and private sector action—simultaneously disrupted two widely used cybercrime-as-a-service platforms, Amadey (malware loader) and StealC (infostealer), by exploiting overlapping infrastructure. Microsoft used AI to identify the shared infrastructure and invoked RICO statutes to treat both tools as a single criminal conspiracy. The operation took down 326 servers, 142 domains, and 200+ command-and-control systems, recovered 27 million stolen credentials, and uncovered $47 million in crypto assets of criminal origin.

themes.theme: AI-Enabled Cybercrime Disruption and Cross-Tool Takedowns

insights.claim: Microsoft used AI analysis to discover that the Amadey malware-as-a-service platform and the StealC infostealer-as-a-service platform, though independently operated, shared underlying infrastructure—enabling attorneys to invoke RICO statutes and treat both as a single criminal conspiracy for simultaneous takedown.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: AI-driven infrastructure fingerprinting is now a viable tool for cybercrime attribution and legal strategy; this approach could be replicated across other cybercrime ecosystems.
insights.quote: Microsoft said it made this determination after analyzing the tools using AI. This insight allowed Microsoft attorneys to seek an order disrupting both at the same time.

insights.claim: Operation Endgame disrupted more than 200 command-and-control servers, severed criminal control of more than 18,000 infected computers, and Europol recovered 27 million stolen login credentials along with $47 million in crypto assets of criminal origin.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: The scale of recovered credentials signals the breadth of prior compromise; enterprises should treat this list as evidence of potential ongoing account takeover exposure.
insights.quote: it disrupted more than 200 command-and-control servers and severed criminal control of more than 18,000 infected computers. Europol... recovered as many as 27 million stolen login credentials and uncovered $47 million worth of 'crypto assets of criminal origin.'

insights.claim: The same operation also disrupted SocGholish, a malware loader linked to the Russian cybercrime group Evil Corp that spreads through compromised WordPress websites by tricking visitors into installing trojanized browser extensions.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: WordPress site owners should treat this as a prompt to audit for SocGholish indicators of compromise and enforce credential hygiene across administrative accounts.
insights.quote: another tool disrupted in Operation Endgame is SocGholish, a malware loader linked to the Russian cybercrime group Evil Corp

insights.claim: The Amadey malware-as-a-service platform has been active since at least 2018 and was observed abusing GitHub infrastructure as recently as the prior year, while StealC harvests credentials, authentication cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, and browser extensions.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: The longevity and infrastructure abuse history of Amadey highlights how cybercrime-as-a-service platforms can persist for years before major disruption; a gap that enterprise security teams should factor into threat modeling.
insights.quote: Amadey has been observed in the wild since at least 2018 and was seen last year abusing GitHub as it collected system information from infected devices
